Name: task_relevant_representation_compression
Title: Task-Relevant Representation Compression: An Information-Theoretic Approach
Short Hypothesis: By framing the compression of neural network representations as an information-theoretic problem, we can derive bounds on the minimal bit-cost required to maintain task performance, revealing insights into the trade-offs between compression efficiency and predictive accuracy.
Related Work: Current literature primarily examines model compression through empirical methods or specific applications (e.g., generative models, speech encoding). While there are connections to rate-distortion theory, few studies explicitly quantify the relationship between representation compression and task performance using rigorous information-theoretic frameworks, limiting the understanding of fundamental trade-offs.
Abstract: This proposal aims to explore the compression of neural network representations through the lens of information theory, specifically focusing on the task-relevant aspects of model representations. We will frame the problem as a rate-distortion challenge, where the objective is to minimize the bit-cost of compressed representations while maintaining a predefined level of task performance. By deriving achievable rates and distortion-rate curves, we will establish a rigorous framework linking the compressibility of neural representations to their effectiveness in downstream tasks. This research will not only advance theoretical knowledge in model compression but also provide practical guidelines for developing efficient neural architectures. We anticipate that our findings will open new avenues for optimizing neural networks in a way that balances computational efficiency with predictive sufficiency.
Experiments: 1. Conduct a series of experiments on benchmark datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, MNIST) to evaluate the performance of various compression techniques (e.g., pruning, quantization) under controlled distortion constraints.

2. Derive distortion-rate curves for different architectures (e.g., CNNs, Transformers) and tasks (e.g., classification, regression) to identify the relationships between representation complexity and task performance.
3. Utilize mutual information estimators to quantify the task-relevant information retained post-compression, comparing it with model accuracy metrics.

4. Implement a systematic framework that varies the compression rate and measures the corresponding task performance (e.g., accuracy, F1 score) to validate theoretical bounds derived from rate-distortion principles.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the difficulty in accurately estimating mutual information and distortion in high-dimensional spaces, which may complicate the interpretation of results. Additionally, there may be challenges in generalizing findings across different architectures and tasks. This proposal will require careful selection of distortion metrics to ensure relevance to downstream tasks.
